diagrams.description: Urban Studies is an interdisciplinary field that investigates the social, spatial, economic, and political dynamics of cities. This flowchart maps its analytical logic: the ultimate goal is to understand urban life, inequalities, and the search for sustainable, just cities. It integrates core foundational concepts (urbanization, urbanism, metropolitan region, gentrification, spatial justice, the right to the city) with major theoretical frameworks—the Chicago School (urban ecology), political economy (Lefebvre, Harvey), feminist and postcolonial urbanism (intersectionality, informality), and assemblage/actor‑network approaches. These are applied to key domains of analysis (urban planning and governance, housing and neighborhoods, mobility and infrastructure, urban ecology and environment, culture and everyday life, immigration and diversity). Diverse methods—qualitative (ethnography, interviews), quantitative (GIS, spatial statistics), archival research, and participatory action research—support the research process. The critical cycle moves from analysis of spatial structures → critique of power and inequality → visioning alternative urban futures → policy and planning interventions. The resulting outcomes include equitable development, sustainable and resilient cities, community empowerment, and deeper urban knowledge.
